policy_statement: Investigate CCS/CCU from energy recovery through waste incineration and, if necessary and appropriate, introduce CCS/CCU in collaboration with other municipalities (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: It investigates adding CCS/CCU to energy recovery from waste incineration and, if needed, introduces it to capture CO₂ from the flue gas and reduce emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste incineration (point source) + CCS/CCU → CO₂ capture → ↓ CO₂ released to atmosphere → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in system/activity: Waste-to-energy incineration plants are retrofitted or designed with carbon capture (and associated compression/transport infrastructure) and potentially CO₂ utilization. (2) Mediating process: Post-combustion capture separates CO₂ from flue gas; CO₂ is compressed and sent to geological storage or to utilization pathways that either store carbon long-term or displace fossil CO₂ production. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced point-source CO₂ emissions (and potentially net-negative emissions for the biogenic fraction of waste if storage is permanent), lowering overall atmospheric CO₂ accumulation.
dominant_pathway_test: Capturing CO₂ from waste-incineration flue gas and permanently storing it (or using it in a way that avoids near-term release) is the single biggest climate-relevant effect.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Point-source engineering control at waste incinerators captures CO₂ that would otherwise be emitted → direct reduction in net CO₂ emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While CCU and energy recovery can have resource-efficiency elements, the mechanism’s core intent and largest climate effect is emissions mitigation via capture and (ideally) permanent storage/avoidance of atmospheric release.  
3) Edge cases applied: If CO₂ is utilized in short-lived products (e.g., beverages, some fuels), mitigation benefits may be limited or temporary; nevertheless, the mechanism is still primarily framed as emissions reduction via capture.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy described as adding CCS/CCU to waste incineration shares the same dominant mitigation pathway of reducing point-source CO₂ emissions from flue gas.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtype distinctions (e.g., carbon removal vs. emissions reduction depending on biogenic share and storage permanence) are deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net climate benefit depends on the fate of captured CO₂ (permanent geological storage vs short-lived utilization), capture rate, added energy use (capture penalty), and the waste’s biogenic vs fossil carbon fraction; these affect magnitude and whether outcomes include net-negative emissions, but not the primary mitigation classification.
key_indicators: "Investigate the introduction of CCS/CCU"; "If necessary and appropriate, CCS/CCU is to be introduced"; "energy recovery through waste incineration"; "in collaboration with other municipalities"
co_benefits: Potential air-pollution co-benefits (if CCS equipment changes co-pollutant controls) and local industrial innovation/jobs, but not explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as infrastructure-investment if the text specified funding/build-out of capture equipment; here it is primarily a feasibility/decision step with conditional implementation, so planning-strategy is the closest fit.
